candidates.item: The product name 'Domesday' deliberately evokes the original 1086 Domesday Book — a comprehensive survey/record of ownership and control — as a brand metaphor for their ownership/compliance data graph.
candidates.why_interesting: Naming a defense-sector compliance product after an 11th-century Norman census of land ownership is a genuinely clever and specific brand choice — it signals domain seriousness and has an implicit thesis ('who owns what, verifiably, on record') baked into the name itself. Most compliance tools pick sterile acronyms or vague trust-words; this one made a historical argument. Worth reacting to on its own terms.
candidates.recency_note: Noted in first-party homepage content; site appears newly launched with no search engine indexing found.

candidates.item: Positioned as graph-native data intelligence, not a simple registry lookup — emphasis on provenance, propagation, and audit trail.
candidates.why_interesting: Most FOCI/beneficial-ownership screening tools are glorified watchlist lookups — you paste in a name and get a hit/no-hit. Building a live, propagating ownership *graph* that pushes updates when control shifts through intermediaries is architecturally distinct and solves the actual hard problem (layered shell structures, mid-contract ownership changes). That's a meaningful technical stance, not just a feature claim.
candidates.recency_note: Noted in first-party homepage content; site appears newly launched with no search engine indexing found.

candidates.item: Ownership graph propagates updates when control shifts through intermediaries — covering suppliers, investors, and adjacent firms — surfacing structural drift and downstream risk so primes, subcontractors, and partners can escalate early.
candidates.why_interesting: The specific phrase 'structural drift' is unusually precise — it names the real adversarial pattern in FOCI risk (incremental, intermediate-layer ownership changes that individually look innocent but cumulatively cross a threshold). Treating it as a first-class trackable event, rather than a one-time point-in-time screen, is a genuinely different product posture.
candidates.recency_note: Noted in first-party homepage content; site appears newly launched with no search engine indexing found.

candidates.item: Compliance & Sanctions product area maps CMMC, ITAR, Section 847, Section 889 and related regimes to evidence bundles and screening outputs, 'with counsel retained where required.'
candidates.why_interesting: The 'counsel retained where required' line is a striking architectural detail — it implies the product doesn't just flag risk but also routes certain review types to legal review as part of the workflow. That's either in-house legal or legal-as-a-service wired into the product, which is genuinely unusual for a data/software platform and suggests they've identified that compliance outputs need defensible legal sign-off, not just software output.
candidates.recency_note: Noted in first-party homepage content; site appears newly launched with no search engine indexing found.

candidates.item: Review Workflows product area supports analyst/operator triage, investigation, and contract-adjacent workflows over 'Domesday's graph and provenance,' with audit logs, escalation paths, and configurable constraints.
candidates.why_interesting: Describing workflows as 'contract-adjacent' is an unusually blunt framing — it openly positions the tool as part of the contracting decision process, not just a background research aid. That's a notable product scope claim: they're saying the output is actionable enough to sit next to a contract, not just inform a risk memo.
candidates.recency_note: Noted in first-party homepage content; site appears newly launched with no search engine indexing found.
